findings.description: The HTML converts the dense, compact table layout into a very tall, single-column layout with excessive vertical spacing. Each row in the HTML takes significantly more vertical space, turning a single-page document into a very long scrolling page.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a compact table fitting on one page with tight row spacing. HTML renders each row with much more vertical padding and the parameter names wrap onto multiple lines, greatly expanding the document length.

findings.description: The table header structure is altered. The PDF has a clear multi-row header with Report #, Lab, and PQL columns grouped logically with dates (2/14/2014, 2/17/2014, 2/26/2014, 2/27/2014) spanning across. The HTML header adds extra sub-columns (Report #, Lab, Lab Dissolved, etc.) that expand the table horizontally in a confusing way.
findings.evidence: PDF header shows clean date groupings with AC04986/AC04987/AC04993 under 2/14/2014, etc. HTML header shows expanded columns with Report #, Lab, Lab Dissolved repeated under each date, creating a wider and more confusing header structure.

findings.description: The qualifier columns (U flags) appear to be separated into their own distinct columns in the HTML rather than being adjacent to their associated values as in the PDF. This changes how data is read and associated.
findings.evidence: PDF shows values like '0.4 U' in compact adjacent cells. HTML separates these into distinct 'Value' and 'Qual' columns under each date grouping.

findings.description: The 'Applicable Water Quality Standard' and 'Basis for Standard' columns appear to be present but the column headers in HTML read differently from the PDF.
findings.evidence: PDF has 'Applicable Water Quality Standard' and 'Basis for Standard'. HTML has similar but layout differs with 'Applicable Water Quality Standard' and 'Basis Standard'.

findings.description: Bold formatting on headers and title text differs. The PDF title 'Data from Ash Release at Duke Energy Dan River Station' is bold and centered. HTML preserves the text but styling appears different.
findings.evidence: PDF shows bold, centered title. HTML shows the title text but with potentially different weight/emphasis.

findings.description: The notes/annotations section ('no NC standards for' and 'dissolved metals') that appear in the rightmost columns of the PDF for Al-Dissolved Aluminum and As-Dissolved Arsenic rows are present but may read differently in the HTML layout.
findings.evidence: PDF shows 'no NC standards for dissolved metals' spanning across the last two columns. HTML shows these as 'no NC standards for' and 'dissolved metals' in separate cells.

findings.description: The 'Applicable Water Quality Standard' and 'Basis for Standard' columns for dissolved metals rows are misrepresented. In the PDF, 'Al - Dissolved Aluminum' and 'As Dissolved Arsenic' share a notation 'no NC standards for dissolved metals' spanning both rows. The HTML duplicates this text across both the Standard and Basis columns for both rows, which is inaccurate.
findings.evidence: PDF shows 'no NC standards for dissolved metals' as a single note spanning the standard area. HTML has both 'Al - Dissolved Aluminum' and 'As Dissolved Arsenic' rows with 'no NC standards for' and 'dissolved metals' split across Standard and Basis columns for both rows.

findings.description: The table header structure is overly complex and not faithful to the original. The PDF has separate header rows for dates, report numbers, and sample IDs. The HTML merges all this information into single column headers, making the structure harder to parse and losing the clear date-based grouping.
findings.evidence: PDF headers: '2/14/2014' row, then 'Report # AC04986', 'Lab AC04987', 'Dissolved AC04993' as sub-headers. HTML combines into single header like '2/14/2014 Report # AC04986 Lab AC04987 Dissolved AC04993 Value'.

findings.description: The HTML splits each date column into 'Value' and 'Qual' sub-columns, creating 8 data columns for 4 dates. While this captures the qualifier flags, the PDF presents qualifiers inline with values (e.g., '0.4 U' in the same cell). This restructuring, while functional, adds column headers ('Value', 'Qual') that do not exist in the PDF.
findings.evidence: PDF: single column per date with values like '0.4 U'. HTML: two columns per date labeled 'Value' and 'Qual' — these column header labels are fabricated.

findings.description:
The HTML has no heading hierarchy at all. The document title and sub-titles are rendered as bold paragraphs ( within

) rather than as headings (h1, h2, etc.).
findings.evidence:
HTML uses '

Data from Ash Release at Duke Energy Dan River Station' instead of an h1 or h2 heading.

findings.description: Parameter names have underscores removed and formatting slightly changed, which is acceptable reformatting but noted. For example 'Residue_Suspended' becomes 'Residue Suspended', 'Aluminum_Al' becomes 'Aluminum Al'.
The column sub-headers 'Value' and 'Qual' do not appear anywhere in the source PDF. They are fabricated labels added during conversion.
findings.evidence: PDF column headers show date and sample IDs only. HTML adds 'Value' and 'Qual' as sub-column labels for each sampling date.
